name: Addington CF
classification_notes: A text-optimised serif with classical proportions and moderate contrast, suited for both body text and display use, consistent with transitional serif conventions.
summary: A text serif in seven weights with full italics, covering Latin and Vietnamese and offering small caps, lining figures and discretionary ligatures throughout.
description: Designed to work at text sizes and scale to display, it covers fourteen styles from Thin to Extra Bold with matching italics. OpenType features include small capitals, tabular and lining figures, ordinals, fractions and a range of discretionary ligatures. Language support is broad for a single-family release, reaching Vietnamese alongside the standard Western European set.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Addington CF as a graceful and reliable serif useful in any application, designed for excellence in text-heavy settings while doubling as a capable, bold display typeface. The family ships in fourteen styles across seven weights with Latin and Vietnamese script support.

name: Almada CF
classification_notes: A text-focused serif drawing on the workhorse office serifs of the 1970s, with long purposeful serifs and carefully tuned spacing suited to extended reading — characteristics typical of transitional or old-style influenced text serifs.
summary: A text serif family with five weights and italics, taking cues from the utilitarian office serifs of the 1970s while adding considered detail for display use.
description: Long, purposeful serifs and careful spacing give Almada CF a steady, legible texture for extended reading, while unexpected details add rhythm at larger sizes. The family spans Extra Light to Extra Bold with matching italics and variable font files, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts across more than thirty languages. It sits comfortably in editorial, document and publishing contexts where reliability matters more than novelty.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Almada CF as a dependable, text-focused serif and a stylish, modern take on the ubiquitous workhorse serifs found in offices around the 1970s. Carefully tuned spacing and long, purposeful serifs provide stability for extended reading, while unexpected details create engaging rhythm at display sizes. The family supports Cyrillic, Greek and Latin scripts and is offered from Extra Light to Extra Bold with italics and variable fonts.

name: Argent CF
classification_notes: A serif with pronounced x-height and flowing, expressive letterforms aimed at headings and titles, placing it firmly in the display serif category rather than a text-optimised style.
summary: A display serif with a pronounced x-height and flowing letterforms, suited to headings, titles and logotypes across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Argent CF comes in seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving 13 styles in total. The design leans expressive and decorative, with charming italics and a solid suite of OpenType features including small capitals, old-style figures and discretionary ligatures. Language support spans Latin and Cyrillic, covering over 30 languages from Belarusian and Bulgarian to Turkish and Uzbek.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Argent CF as dashing and expressive, with a pronounced x-height and evocative, flowing letterforms. They highlight its charming italics, wide language support and host of OpenType features, positioning it as a serif family that excels at headings, titles and logos.

name: Argent Pixel CF
classification_notes: A bitmap interpretation of the serif typeface Argent, designed to evoke early Macintosh low-resolution typography; the pixelated construction and retro aesthetic place it firmly in the display category despite functional serif origins.
summary: A bitmap adaptation of the Argent serif, redrawn in a pixelated style reminiscent of early Macintosh screen typography, with angled serifs and swashy italics.
description: Taking its cues from the original Argent typeface, this pixel version recreates the family's distinctive proportions and angled serifs within a low-resolution grid. The result recalls the character of early Macintosh system fonts whilst remaining legible enough for a range of print and screen applications. Two styles, Regular and Regular Italic, cover extended Latin for branding, editorial, book covers and packaging work that calls for a retro aesthetic.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Argent Pixel CF as a playful bitmap take on the original Argent typeface, noting its pronounced x-height and unexpectedly strong readability despite the lo-fi construction. They highlight the angled serifs and swashy italics as particularly distinctive, and position the family for retro-flavoured work across identities, advertising, magazines, book covers and packaging.

name: Articulat CF
classification_notes: Described as a take on Swiss typography, Articulat aligns with the grotesque tradition characterised by strong stroke contrast, rational structure and mid-century reference points.
summary: A Swiss-influenced grotesque with ten weights and obliques, covering Latin and Cyrillic with broad language support including Vietnamese and several Eastern European scripts.
description: Articulat CF takes the rational structure of mid-20th-century Swiss grotesques and adds enough variation through stylistic sets to avoid feeling anonymous. Twenty styles span Thin through Heavy with matching obliques, and the character set stretches across Latin and Cyrillic to cover over 30 languages. Stylistic alternates for characters like 'a', 'g' and 'l' give designers control over how geometric or humanist the texture reads.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Articulat CF as a new take on timeless Swiss typography — strong, sharp and versatile, built for legibility with mid-century character reimagined for the 21st century. The family ships with ten weights in Latin and Cyrillic, stylistic sets and script alternates.

name: Artifex CF
classification_notes: Described as a humanist sans-serif with a calligraphic flare replacing traditional serifs, lending it warmth and moderate contrast typical of the humanist genre.
summary: A humanist sans with flared terminals that give it a calligraphic warmth, spanning eight weights with broad Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: Artifex Hand CF replaces conventional serifs with a subtle terminal flare, sitting between a pure sans and a semi-serif and lending running text a calligraphic rhythm. Eight weights from Extra Light to Heavy each carry matching italics, and the character set extends to Cyrillic, Greek, Vietnamese, Pinyin and IPA, covering over thirty languages. The moderate contrast and even texture make it serviceable across both text and display sizes.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Artifex Hand CF as a humanist sans-serif variation on the original Artifex typeface, with serifs replaced by a subtle flare to create a calligraphic feel and warm tone. Like its sibling, it is designed for flowing, easy-to-read text while doubling as a display font, available in eight weights across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Vietnamese, Pinyin and IPA script sets.

name: Artifex Hand CF
classification_notes: A flared sans derived from its serif sibling Artifex, with calligraphic stroke endings in place of conventional serifs, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition with warm, pen-derived detailing.
summary: A flared sans variant of Artifex CF, trading serifs for calligraphic stroke endings across eight weights with broad Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage.
description: Derived from Connary Fagen's Artifex CF serif, this family replaces conventional serifs with subtle flares that retain a calligraphic warmth without tipping into script territory. Sixteen styles span Extra Light to Heavy with true italics throughout, and the glyph set extends to Vietnamese, Pinyin, IPA and multiple romanisation sets. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and discretionary ligatures, making it suited to continuous text as well as display use.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describes Artifex Hand CF as a humanist sans-serif variation on the original Artifex typeface, with serifs replaced by subtle flares that lend a calligraphic feel and warm tone. The family is designed for flowing, easy-to-read text while doubling as a display font, and includes extensive Latin, Cyrillic and Greek support, IPA glyphs, transliteration sets for Sanskrit, Arabic and Pinyin, and a full suite of OpenType features.

name: Cartograph CF
classification_notes: A monospaced family explicitly designed for code and terminal use, with a cursive italic and warmth drawn from humanist traditions rather than strict geometric or mechanical mono conventions.
summary: A warm, character-rich monospaced family built for both code and prose, with a lush cursive italic and extensive OpenType features including code ligatures.
description: Eight weights with matching italics span from Thin to Heavy, making it unusually expressive for a coding font. The italic is genuinely cursive rather than a slanted roman, and a proportional alternate set is accessible via OpenType for prose contexts. Language support is broad, covering Latin Extended, Cyrillic, Greek, Katakana and Vietnamese, with a contextual ligature system tailored to programming syntax.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Cartograph CF as a monospaced typeface with character and warmth, designed as a tribute to the utilitarian beauty of terminals and typewriters. They highlight its lush cursive italic, code-friendly ligatures and proportional OpenType set, positioning it as equally at home in code editors and long-form text.

name: Cerulya CF
classification_notes: Cerulya is explicitly a display typeface with a semi-serif construction, featuring rounded teardrop terminals, swashes and decorative alternates that place it firmly in the display category rather than conventional serif or sans-serif.
summary: A semi-serif display typeface with rounded teardrop terminals, swashes and an extensive set of alternates and ligatures, built for large-scale use.
description: Spanning six weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, Cerulya CF sits between a serif and a script in character, its rounded teardrop terminals and flowing stroke contrast giving it movement without fully committing to either category. The family ships with discretionary ligatures, swashes and numerous stylistic sets, making it well-suited to branding, poster work and editorial headlines where typographic detail rewards scrutiny. Latin and Vietnamese are covered across a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Cerulya CF as airy and delicate, designed to shine at large sizes for logos, posters and headlines. They highlight its semi-serif construction with rounded teardrop terminals and note the inclusion of ligatures, swashes and alternates, recommending it paired with a contrasting geometric sans such as Greycliff CF at dramatically different point sizes.

name: Couplet CF
classification_notes: Couplet is built on a strong rectangular, slightly condensed geometric construction with occasional corner rounding that softens its otherwise strict geometry, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A slightly condensed geometric sans in seven weights, with corner rounding that softens an otherwise rectangular construction across a wide range of scripts.
description: Built on a strict rectangular geometry with a slightly condensed stance, the typeface tempers its precision with occasional rounded corners that introduce a subtle warmth without compromising the clean, upright character of the letterforms. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Light to Heavy, each with matching italics, making it a practical choice for both body and display use. Language support extends across Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Vietnamese and Pinyin, broadening its utility for international work.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Couplet CF as expressive and practical, combining precision with warmth. A strong rectangular underpinning and slightly condensed construction support bright, attractive letterforms across seven weights, with occasional corner rounding that adds unexpected softness and subtlety to an otherwise strict geometry. The family was designed for use anywhere, in digital or print contexts, at large or small sizes.

name: Criteria CF
classification_notes: Built on simple geometric construction using straight lines and clean circles, with explicit Swiss style influences placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A nine-weight geometric sans from Connary Fagen, built on circles and straight lines with Swiss style touches and oblique companions across every weight.
description: Eighteen styles span nine weights from Thin to Black, each paired with an oblique, covering Latin and Vietnamese script. Construction is deliberately simple and efficient, using clean circles and straight lines, though stylistic sets for alternate letterforms add some flexibility. Aimed at stacked body text, display headlines and wordmarks, it sits in familiar geometric territory without straying far from convention.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Criteria CF as a geometric sans built with simple, efficient construction, combining straight lines and clean circles with a thoughtful design suited to cleanly-stacked lowercase text, striking headlines and bold wordmarks. They note hints of Swiss style and unconventional ideas that add a fresh dimension to an otherwise strong geometric design, with nine weights and language support spanning Latin and Vietnamese across 28 languages.

name: Custom Sans CF
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes it as a geometric sans serif with a large x-height, even rhythm and open shapes, characteristic of the geometric tradition.
summary: A geometric sans in nine weights with matching italics, built for legibility across sizes and suited to branding, packaging and interface work.
description: Even rhythm, open shapes and a large x-height give this workhorse geometric a reliable presence at text sizes without sacrificing usefulness at display scale. Nine weights run from Hairline to Black, each with a matching italic, and the family is also available as two variable fonts. Language support covers a broad range of Latin-script languages including Turkish, Polish and Vietnamese-adjacent extended Latin characters.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Custom Sans CF as a workhorse geometric sans serif designed for reliable performance, with even rhythm and open shapes that prioritise legibility across sizes. It balances casual confidence with universal clarity and works well in both text and display contexts. Suited to branding, packaging, publishing and interface design, it is available in nine weights from Hairline to Black with matching italics.

name: Cygnet
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display typeface, Cygnet features a tall x-height and expressive warmth that positions it firmly in the display sans category rather than a workhorse grotesque or humanist sans.
summary: A nine-weight display sans with an unusually tall x-height, covering Latin and Vietnamese script across 18 styles.
description: Cygnet pairs a generous x-height with a warm, slightly characterful approach to sans-serif forms, making it useful for headlines, captions and logotype work where readability at display sizes matters. Nine weights run from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, covering Latin Extended and Vietnamese. Stylistic alternates for the ampersand, 'b', 'g' and 'y' offer typographic flexibility.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Cygnet CF as charming with classic warmth and curious verve, noting that its absurdly generous x-height makes it a surprisingly readable and versatile display typeface suited to headlines, captions, logos and more. The family covers nine weights in Latin and Vietnamese script with broad European language support.

name: Ellograph CF
classification_notes: Ellograph CF is explicitly described as a monospaced sans; its soft, rounded construction and cursive italics sit within the humanist end of the monospaced genre, suited to both coding and display use.
summary: A rounded monospaced sans in eight weights with striking cursive italics, equally at home in a code editor and on a logo.
description: Soft terminals and a generous x-height give Ellograph CF a cheerful character that sets it apart from purely utilitarian coding fonts. The cursive italics are a deliberate contrast to the upright's restrained geometry, lending versatility across branding, headlines and on-screen interfaces. Code ligatures, Chinese Pinyin and broad Latin Extended coverage broaden its reach considerably for a monospaced family.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Ellograph CF as a cheerful monospaced sans with soft, rounded construction and striking cursive italics. A generous x-height and short descenders produce even, flowing text lines, making it a strong coding font candidate, while designers can also deploy it in logos, headlines, brands, and print. The family covers Latin, Vietnamese and Chinese Pinyin in eight weights, and includes code ligatures.

name: Filter
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an experimental display typeface fusing constructivism, art deco, geometry and science fiction, making it firmly a display face with geometric underpinnings rather than a functional text family.
summary: An experimental display typeface in five weights drawing on constructivism, art deco and science fiction aesthetics, with Latin and Cyrillic support.
description: Five weights from Thin to Bold, designed for short bursts of highly stylised text rather than extended setting. The letterforms pull from constructivist geometry and art deco conventions, with a science fiction inflection that lends it a distinctive retro-futurist character. Language support spans over thirty Latin-script languages plus Belarusian, Bulgarian, Russian, Ukrainian and other Cyrillic-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Filter as an experimental display typeface fusing constructivism, fashion and design across five weights. They note it is suited to short bursts of stylised text and cite influences including art deco, geometry and science fiction, with coverage of Latin and Cyrillic alphabets across more than thirty languages.

name: Greycliff CF
classification_notes: Near-monoline construction with geometric underpinning places it firmly in the geometric sans category, though softened terminals and warm stroke modulation distinguish it from strictly mechanical geometric designs.
summary: A geometric near-monoline sans in nine weights with broad multilingual coverage spanning Latin, Cyrillic, Greek and Vietnamese.
description: Built on a geometric, near-monoline skeleton, Greycliff CF tempers its classic construction with softened edges that give it a warmer quality than strictly mechanical geometric faces. The family runs across nine weights from Thin to Heavy, each with an oblique companion for a total of 18 styles. Language support is unusually wide, covering Belarusian, Bulgarian, Greek, Ukrainian, Vietnamese, Pinyin and over 30 additional languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff CF as rugged, hearty and warm, with strong capitals and a smooth, open lowercase suited to a variety of applications. Its geometric, near-monoline construction is said to lend the family a classic durability reminiscent of the 1940s, softened by rounded edges and vibrant shapes.

name: Greycliff Arabic CF
classification_notes: Greycliff Arabic CF is a geometric sans-serif, extending the rounded, geometric Latin design of Greycliff CF into the Arabic script with matching optical weight and character across both writing systems.
summary: A multiscript extension of Greycliff CF that brings the same soft geometric construction to Arabic, Persian and Pashto alongside Latin.
description: Nine weights span both Latin and Arabic scripts, keeping optical weight and geometric character consistent across writing systems. Arabic diacritics are included alongside broad language support covering Modern Standard Arabic, Persian, Pashto and Urdu, as well as an extensive Latin range for European languages. Designed as a self-contained type system, it suits multilingual branding, app interfaces and editorial work where the two scripts must coexist without visual conflict.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Arabic CF as an adaptation of Greycliff's soft, geometric design to the Arabic script, with both Latin and Arabic glyphs included for visually cohesive multiple-script applications. The family covers Greycliff's original nine weights alongside Arabic diacritics and language support for Modern Standard Arabic, Persian, Pashto and more, functioning as a complete, self-contained type system.

name: Greycliff Devanagari
classification_notes: A geometric, monolinear sans serif adapted from the original Greycliff Latin family into Devanagari script, maintaining the same monolinear, geometric character across both scripts.
summary: A geometric monolinear sans serif extending the Greycliff family into Devanagari script for cohesive Latin and Indic multilingual typesetting.
description: Adapted directly from the original Greycliff typeface, this Devanagari companion preserves the geometric, monolinear character of the Latin across nine weights from Thin to Heavy. Script coverage spans Hindi, Marathi, Nepali and Sanskrit alongside broad Latin extended support for over two dozen languages. There are no italics or condensed cuts, keeping the family tightly focused on multilingual body and UI applications.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Devanagari CF as an adaptation of the original Greycliff typeface to the Devanagari script, recreating the geometric, monolinear design of the original sans serif in full to allow cohesive multilingual applications between English and Hindi, Marathi, Nepali, Sanskrit and more, available in nine weights.

name: Greycliff Gurmukhi CF
classification_notes: Near-monoline construction with geometric forms and softened terminals places this squarely in the geometric sans category, covering both Latin and Gurmukhi scripts.
summary: A geometric sans covering both Gurmukhi and Latin scripts across nine weights, designed for visually consistent multi-script typography in Panjabi and a broad range of Latin languages.
description: Built on a near-monoline geometric construction with softened edges, the family pairs Latin letterforms with a full Gurmukhi character set including diacritics and subscript letters for Panjabi language support. Nine weights from Thin to Heavy give it range across text and display applications. Latin language coverage spans over two dozen languages including Croatian, Turkish and Filipino.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Gurmukhi CF as rugged, hearty and warm, a versatile family covering both Gurmukhi and Latin scripts for visually cohesive multi-script applications. The geometric, near-monoline construction is tempered by softened edges and vibrant shapes, and the family includes diacritics and subscript letters for full Panjabi language support.

name: Greycliff Hebrew CF
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif sharing the soft rounded construction of the original Greycliff CF, extended to cover the Hebrew script alongside Latin with full diacritics and cantillation marks.
summary: A geometric sans-serif that extends the soft, rounded forms of Greycliff CF into the Hebrew script, covering nine weights with full diacritics and cantillation marks alongside Latin.
description: Designed to function as a self-contained bilingual type system, Greycliff Hebrew CF pairs Hebrew and Latin glyphs drawn to complement one another in weight and optical rhythm across nine styles from Thin to Heavy. The Hebrew coverage includes diacritics, cantillation marks and wide language support across the script. Latin support extends to a broad range of European languages via extensive diacritic coverage.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Hebrew CF as an adaptation of Greycliff's soft geometric design to the Hebrew script, with both Latin and Hebrew included for visually cohesive multiple-script applications. The family covers Greycliff's original nine weights alongside diacritics, cantillation marks and broad Hebrew-script language support, and is positioned as a complete, self-contained type system.

name: Greycliff Japanese CF
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with warm proportions, extending the original Greycliff CF Latin design into Japanese script, maintaining consistent geometric construction across hiragana, katakana, kanji and romaji.
summary: A Japanese-script extension of Greycliff CF, covering hiragana, katakana and kanji across nine weights for dual-language Latin and Japanese setting.
description: All 2800-plus glyphs were hand-drawn to carry the warm, geometric character of the original Greycliff CF into Japanese script, covering hiragana, katakana, kanji and romaji. Nine weights from Thin to Heavy run consistently across both Latin and Japanese, making it practical for bilingual applications in branding, publishing or interface work. Latin coverage extends to a broad range of European languages including Vietnamese and Filipino.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Japanese CF as a Japanese-script adaptation of the original Greycliff CF sans serif, retaining its warm geometric design. The family spans nine weights covering hiragana, katakana, kanji and romaji, with all 2800-plus glyphs hand-drawn. Future updates are planned to add further kanji, including rare name and geographic glyphs.

name: Greycliff Thai CF
classification_notes: Greycliff Thai is a geometric sans-serif adapted from the Greycliff CF family, characterised by soft, rounded geometry applied consistently across both Latin and Thai scripts.
summary: A soft geometric sans extending the Greycliff CF family into Thai script, with nine weights and obliques covering both Latin and Thai in a single cohesive system.
description: Greycliff Thai CF carries the rounded, geometric character of Connary Fagen's original Greycliff across two scripts, pairing Latin and Thai glyphs designed to sit comfortably alongside one another. The family runs to 18 styles across nine weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching oblique. Full Thai diacritics are included, making it a self-contained solution for bilingual Latin-Thai applications in branding, publishing and digital products.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Greycliff Thai CF as an adaptation of Greycliff's soft, geometric design for the Thai script, with both Latin and Thai glyphs included for cohesive multiple-script use. The family offers nine weights with obliques and full Thai diacritics, and is presented as a complete, self-contained type system where both scripts are designed to complement one another.

name: Gryffith
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as blending sans-serif form with handwriting rhythm and blackletter heft, placing it firmly in the display category rather than a functional text sans.
summary: A display sans from Connary Fagen that folds blackletter weight and handwriting rhythm into upright letterforms across seven weights.
description: Seven weights with matching italics give the family range from editorial headlines to heavy poster work. The letterforms draw their heft from blackletter tradition while the overall structure stays within a sans-serif framework, producing a distinctive tension suited to branding and display applications. Latin script support spans the major Western European languages including Czech, Polish, Turkish and Romanian.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Gryffith as a display typeface that merges the form of a sans-serif with the rhythm of handwriting and the weight of blackletter, recommending it for logos, headlines and posters. The family ships in seven weights with italics, covering Latin script with broad European language support.

name: Hexaframe CF
classification_notes: Built from hard polygons and rounded edges with a mechanical, industrial character, Hexaframe is clearly a display typeface designed for visual impact rather than extended reading, placing it firmly in geometric display territory.
summary: A hard-edged geometric display face built from polygonal forms and rounded corners, aimed at STEM, robotics and corporate identity work.
description: Seven weights from Thin to Heavy, each with an oblique companion, give the family range across branding and interface applications. The letterforms draw on industrial and mechanical aesthetics, making it a reasonable fit for technology branding, STEM products and UI contexts where a robust technical personality is wanted. Latin script coverage spans a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Hexaframe CF as evoking the awe and potential of heavy machinery and robotics, clad in tough polygons and rounded edges. They position it as a typeface for corporate identity, STEM toys and user interface design, with seven weights in Latin and stylistic sets, plus broad European language support.

name: Integral CF
classification_notes: Integral CF uses straight lines and angular geometry with a corporate, display-oriented construction typical of geometric sans-serif typefaces, designed for impact at large sizes.
summary: A six-weight geometric sans built for display use, running from Regular to Heavy with matching obliques across 12 styles.
description: Straight-lined and confident in construction, it is aimed at posters, social media graphics, headlines and large-format print where visual weight matters. The foundry notes a streak of character beneath the corporate geometry, suggesting it sits between pure utility and personality. Language support spans a broad range of Latin-based European languages including French, German, Spanish and Turkish.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Integral CF as designed for maximum visual and emotional impact, with six weights suited to posters, social media, headlines and large-format print. They note that straight lines and corporate confidence conceal a hint of roguish charm, and that the family lends words a strong voice while remaining enjoyable to use.

name: Manifold CF
classification_notes: A utilitarian geometric sans with terminal-inspired precision and a tall x-height, positioned for interfaces and technical applications. The page describes it as merging engineering clarity with a warm, unified structure.
summary: A utilitarian geometric sans with a tall x-height and engineering precision, available in eight weights across standard and extended widths with variable font support.
description: Built around terminal-inspired forms and a tall x-height, it suits interface design, short copy and, when tracked out, cinematic headlines. The Extended subfamily widens the proportions into confident, broad forms suited to tech branding, sports and bold editorial. Thirty-two styles in total, including four variable fonts, with coverage across Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Vietnamese spanning more than thirty languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Manifold CF as a utilitarian sans serif that merges terminal-inspired precision with a warm, unified structure and a tall x-height. They recommend it for interfaces, short copy and cinematic titles when tracked out, with the Extended style pushing the engineering aesthetic further for tech, sports, editorial and bold branding. The superfamily spans eight weights with matching obliques, Latin, Cyrillic and Vietnamese support, and more than thirty languages.

name: Mezzotint CF
classification_notes: A bold display serif with high-contrast strokes, flared serifs and inky terminals; intended for logotypes, posters and editorial use rather than extended text setting.
summary: A single-weight bold display serif with high-contrast strokes, flared serifs and inky terminals, aimed at logotypes, posters and editorial headings.
description: Available in Regular and Regular Italic, the family relies on contrast and character rather than a weight range to create impact. Aqueous stroke connections and a generous set of OpenType alternates, swashes and discretionary ligatures give designers room to customise letterforms for logotype work. Language support covers Latin Extended and Vietnamese, spanning over two dozen languages from Catalan to Turkish.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Mezzotint CF as a daring and playful display typeface built for logotypes, posters and editorial use. High-contrast strokes, flared serifs, aqueous connections and inky terminals define its striking, rakish personality. The family ships with roman and italic sets, wide language support, optional ligatures, OpenType alternates and more, and Connary Fagen suggest pairing it with a contrasting geometric sans such as Greycliff CF at a large size differential for maximum effect.

name: Mielle CF
classification_notes: A connected cursive script with a near-monolinear stroke, drawn to evoke casual handwritten lettering. The flowing joins, swash alternates and stylistic sets confirm its classification as a cursive script typeface.
summary: A near-monolinear cursive script with subtle stroke modulation, designed for logos, signage and casual branding work.
description: Six weights from Light to Heavy are available alongside a variable font, with the near-monolinear strokes giving the family a clean, honey-like warmth. Ligatures, contextual alternates and multiple stylistic sets allow for more convincingly handwritten settings. Language support covers Latin Extended and Vietnamese, spanning over two dozen languages including Catalan, Polish and Turkish.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Mielle CF as a playful cursive script appearing as if drawn with warm honey. The design reads as monolinear but carries subtle stroke variation to achieve balanced, even shapes. The family suits charming and casual projects such as logos, signage, posters and branding, with ligatures and alternates adding a more handwritten character. They suggest pairing it with simpler typefaces that won't compete for attention.

name: Novaletra Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist design, with brush-informed construction, subtle flares and low stroke contrast that differentiate it from neutral or geometric sans families.
summary: A humanist sans with brush-informed construction and subtle flares, designed as a companion to Novaletra Serif for longform reading across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
description: Subtle flares and low stroke contrast give this sans-serif a warmth typically absent from the genre, drawing on brushstroke geometry to soften the letterforms without disrupting reading rhythm. Fourteen styles span seven weights from Light to Heavy, each with a matching italic, making it a practical choice for books, newspapers and articles. Language support covers Latin Extended with Vietnamese, plus a full Cyrillic set encompassing Belarusian, Bulgarian, Ukrainian, Serbian and several Central Asian languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Novaletra Sans as informed by smooth brushstrokes, positioning it as a sister typeface to Novaletra Serif. They highlight its easy readability and humanist design, noting that subtle flares and low stroke contrast provide warmth uncommon in sans-serif families, with the family suited to longform texts, books, articles and newspapers. It includes Latin and Cyrillic glyphs in both upright and italic sets across seven weights.

name: Novaletra Serif CF
classification_notes: Low contrast, smooth letterforms with broad weight range suggest a humanist serif designed for multi-purpose use across text and display sizes rather than a classical high-contrast model.
summary: A low-contrast humanist serif spanning seven weights with italics, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Vietnamese scripts for body text and headline use.
description: Designed for legibility across a wide range of sizes, Novaletra Serif CF offers 14 styles from Light to Heavy, each with a matching italic. The low-contrast construction keeps it readable at small text sizes while the weight range gives it sufficient range for headlines. Language support is broad, taking in Belarusian, Bulgarian, Ukrainian, Vietnamese and over 30 other languages across Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Novaletra Serif CF as combining legibility, flexibility and personality across seven weights with italics, covering Latin and Cyrillic scripts. They highlight features including wide language support, international currency symbols, fractions and tabular numbers, positioning it as a versatile choice for body text, captions and headlines.

name: Olivette
classification_notes: Olivette features extreme stroke contrast with wide, open construction, placing it firmly in the high-contrast serif tradition suited to display and large-size use. Its combination of fine hairlines and broad strokes aligns with a contemporary didone sensibility.
summary: A high-contrast serif with extreme stroke variation and wide, open letterforms, designed for large-size display use on screen and in print.
description: Extreme stroke contrast and spacious construction give Olivette a distinctly elegant character at headline sizes, where its fine hairline details can be appreciated. The family spans six weights from Light to Heavy, each with a matching italic, covering broad Latin script support across 25 languages. Connary Fagen suggest pairing it with simpler typefaces such as Greycliff CF at smaller text sizes, letting Olivette occupy generous white space.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Olivette as blending understated minimalism with elegance and expression, with fine details that shine at large sizes on screen and in print. They recommend setting it large with plenty of open space and pairing it with simpler faces like Greycliff CF or Artifex Hand CF. The family includes six weights with italic companions and supports an extensive range of Latin languages including stylistic set script alternates.

name: Quincy CF
classification_notes: Medium contrast with warm, humanist qualities places Quincy in the transitional serif category, though its flowing rhythm and approachable character give it a contemporary feel suited to both text and display use.
summary: A warm, medium-contrast serif in eight weights with Cyrillic and Vietnamese support, suited to both running text and display settings.
description: Quincy CF offers medium contrast and humanist touches that give text a flowing, readable quality across its sixteen styles. The heaviest weights carry enough presence for headlines and medium-scale display work, while the lighter cuts and dedicated Text weight serve body copy. Language support spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Vietnamese, covering over thirty languages including Belarusian, Ukrainian and Serbian.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Quincy CF as having warm letterforms and medium contrast that give text a smooth, flowing motion. Small variations and human touches add charm, and the boldest weights are highlighted as especially strong for large and medium display type. The family ships in eight weights with Latin, Vietnamese and Cyrillic script support.

name: Quiverleaf CF
classification_notes: The foundry describes a ribbon-like flow from letter to letter with graceful movement, characteristic of a connected script typeface. The cursive italics and flowing forms confirm this classification.
summary: A flowing connected script from Connary Fagen spanning five weights with matching italics and broad Latin and Vietnamese coverage.
description: Described by the foundry as moving with the grace of a ribbon, the letterforms link continuously across five weights from Light to Extra Bold, each paired with a matching italic. Capital ligatures and stylistic sets add flexibility for display and editorial applications. Language support extends across Latin-based European scripts and Vietnamese, making it broadly usable across multilingual projects.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Quiverleaf as gliding effortlessly from letter to letter with the graceful movement of a ribbon. Five weights from delicate thin to hearty extra bold combine with striking italics and wide language support, making it suitable for both text setting and airy headlines.

name: Quiverleaf Arabic
classification_notes: Described as humanist in origin, the family carries flowing, ribbon-like strokes across both Latin and Arabic scripts, characteristic of humanist sans design adapted for multi-script use.
summary: A humanist sans that extends the Latin Quiverleaf family into Arabic script, offering five weights for both scripts in a visually unified family.
description: The ribbon-like stroke movement of the original Quiverleaf carries across into Arabic letterforms, maintaining visual cohesion between the two scripts. Five weights from Light to Extra Bold suit both running text and large-scale headline use. Language support spans Arabic, Persian and Urdu alongside broad Latin Extended coverage including Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Quiverleaf Arabic CF as bringing the graceful flowing movement of the original humanist Quiverleaf to Arabic script, with shapes appearing ribbon-like and effortlessly connected. They position its versatile design as suited to text and headlines alike, recommending it for print packaging, global brands and oversized posters across five weights in Latin and Arabic.

name: Roxborough CF
classification_notes: A high-contrast serif with calligraphic influences, a distinctive single-storey 'a', and expressive italics — oriented towards display, branding and packaging rather than extended text setting.
summary: A high-contrast display serif with calligraphic roots and a signature single-storey 'a', aimed at branding, packaging and editorial work.
description: Eight weights with matching italics give Roxborough CF a wide range for display and identity applications. The design draws on calligraphy and hand lettering, with an open construction and stylised italics that depart noticeably from the upright. Latin and Cyrillic coverage extends to Vietnamese and a broad roster of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Roxborough CF as a dramatic, voguish serif influenced by calligraphy and hand lettering. They highlight its open construction and distinctive single-storey 'a', alongside expressive italics, and position it across branding, identity, display, packaging, subtitles and copy.

name: Turismo CF
classification_notes: Strong rectangular shapes combined with sloping elongated curves inspired by midcentury motorsports and technology place this firmly in the display category with a geometric constructivist character.
summary: A seven-weight display sans drawn from midcentury motorsports and technology aesthetics, combining rectangular structures with elongated sloping curves for logotypes and headlines.
description: Turismo CF draws on 1920s and 1960s references to build a geometric display sans suited to logotype and headline work. Rectangular forms sit alongside sloping, elongated curves that give the face a period character without direct revival. Seven weights from Light to Heavy cover Latin and Cyrillic script with broad language support across European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Turismo CF as inspired by midcentury motorsports, technology, and business, designed for stunning logotypes and gripping headlines. It takes cues from both the 1960s and 1920s, combining strong rectangular shapes with sloping, elongated curves, and is available in seven weights with Latin and Cyrillic script support.

name: Vanguard CF
classification_notes: Connary Fagen describe it as a display typeface constructed to maximise horizontal space, with a geometric, condensed character well suited to headlines and titles rather than body text.
summary: A wide, geometric display typeface in eight weights with obliques, built to hold its own in headlines, video and social media.
description: Originally sketched in 2012, Vanguard CF spans Thin to Heavy across sixteen styles, each weight accompanied by an oblique. Its construction prioritises horizontal presence, making it a natural fit for large-scale print, broadcast titles and social graphics. Stylistic sets offer alternate forms for select characters, and language support extends across Latin scripts including Vietnamese.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Vanguard CF as a powerful and elegant display typeface built to maximise horizontal space. Originating from sketches drawn in 2012, it covers eight weights from Thin to Heavy with matching obliques, and draws comparisons to its sibling Integral CF for its strong impression across print, headlines, video and social media.

name: Venti
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric font family, but with subtle humanist warmth in its letterforms, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category with soft humanist overtones.
summary: A geometric sans with soft humanist warmth across eight weights, covering Latin and Cyrillic for headlines, logos and short text.
description: Sixteen styles spanning eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, give this geometric sans plenty of range for branding and editorial work. The foundry describes subtle human overtones that temper the geometry, making it feel approachable rather than clinical. Language support extends to over 30 scripts including Cyrillic, covering Belarusian, Bulgarian, Ukrainian and Serbian among others.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Venti as a geometric font family with a warm, engaging character, noting that its subtle human overtones make it inviting and versatile, well suited to headlines, logos and short text. The family ships in eight weights with Latin and Cyrillic support across more than 30 languages.

name: Visby CF
classification_notes: Visby CF is a geometric sans with hard lines, sharp corners and rounded forms, explicitly described as geometric in its construction with subtle humanist warmth added through lowercase forms.
summary: A geometric sans with sharp corners and rounded forms, drawing on Arctic imagery and carrying subtle humanist warmth across eight weights with Cyrillic and Vietnamese support.
description: Visby CF pairs hard geometric construction with softened, rounded details and oblique styles rather than true italics, spanning eight weights from Thin to Heavy. The lowercase reads as approachable while the uppercase carries a more authoritative tone, making the family versatile across branding and editorial contexts. Language coverage is broad, encompassing Latin Extended, Vietnamese and Cyrillic scripts including Belarusian, Bulgarian, Russian, Ukrainian and Serbian.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Visby CF as friendly and charismatic in lowercase, sophisticated and authoritative in uppercase, inspired by the stark beauty and crisp air of the Arctic North. Hard lines and sharp corners combine with smooth, rounded forms, while subtle humanist influences add warmth. The family ships in sixteen styles across eight weights with Latin, Vietnamese and Cyrillic script support.

name: Visby Round
classification_notes: A rounded geometric sans with humanist touches, described as a sibling to the Visby family. The rounded terminals and overall geometry place it firmly in the rounded geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A rounded geometric sans in eight weights with obliques, covering Latin and Cyrillic for friendly, approachable typesetting.
description: Derived from Connary Fagen's Visby, this variant softens the geometric forms with rounded terminals and subtle humanist influences. Sixteen styles span Extra Light through Heavy, each with a matching oblique, and the character set covers over thirty languages in Latin and Cyrillic scripts. The warmth of the rounded forms suits app interfaces, packaging and display work where a clean geometry would otherwise feel cold.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Visby Round as friendly and charismatic in lowercase, sophisticated and authoritative in uppercase. They position it as a softened sibling of Visby, with rounded forms and subtle humanist influences adding warmth, and offer eight weights across Latin and Cyrillic script support spanning more than thirty languages.

name: Visby Slab
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric slab serif built on the geometric sans Visby family, with circular forms and uniform stroke weight characteristic of the geometric slab genre.
summary: A geometric slab serif companion to the Visby sans family, spanning eight weights with obliques for headlines and display use.
description: Built directly on the foundations of Connary Fagen's Visby sans, this slab version carries the same geometric structure into a bolder, bracketed-serif format across sixteen styles. The eight weights run from Thin to Heavy, each with a corresponding oblique, making it versatile across headlines, captions and short-form text. Language support covers a broad sweep of Latin-script languages including Central and Northern European sets.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Visby Slab as a geometric slab serif built on the popular Visby font family, characterising it as fun, expressive, vibrant and strong across all eight weights. They highlight true obliques, generous language support and suitability for headlines, captions and everything in between.

name: Waverly CF
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as combining art deco tone and flow with modernised construction and variable letter widths, placing it firmly in decorative display territory with period stylistic reference.
summary: An art deco-influenced display typeface with varied letter widths, swash alternates and broad Latin and Cyrillic language coverage across seven weights.
description: Drawing on art deco letterform conventions while updating their construction for contemporary use, it uses deliberate variation in letter width to build rhythm across words, making it well-suited to logotypes and headline artwork. Seven weights from Thin to Heavy are supported, with multiple stylistic sets offering swashes and alternate forms for a range of expressive options. Coverage extends to Belarusian, Bulgarian, Ukrainian, Vietnamese and a wide range of other Latin and Cyrillic-based languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Waverly CF as combining the tone and flow of art deco with updated, clean letterforms and a modernised construction. They note that variation in letter width creates a pleasant rhythm suited to artwork and logos, and highlight broad language support across Latin and Cyrillic scripts alongside a range of swashes and alternate letterforms.

name: Wayfinder CF
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a display font family for logos, headlines and titles, with warm, elegant letterforms and swash alternates suggesting a calligraphic serif influence suited to short text at large sizes.
summary: A seven-weight display serif with swash alternates, built for logos, headlines and short texts where warmth and intensity are both required.
description: Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, giving plenty of range for expressive headline work. Swash capitals, stylistic alternates for a, g, i and r, and oldstyle figures add typographic depth for branding and editorial contexts. Latin extended coverage reaches across most Western and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Wayfinder CF as imparting both elegance and urgency to any text, calling it equally warm and intense. Positioned as a display family for logos, headlines and titles, it spans seven weights from airy thin to heavyweight, with language support across a wide range of Latin-based languages and stylistic sets including swash characters.

name: Wayfinder Stencil
classification_notes: A high-contrast stencil serif with dramatic curves, positioned explicitly for headlines, branding and packaging rather than continuous text. The stencil cuts and strong contrast place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A high-contrast stencil serif in seven weights with italics, aimed at fashion, tech and packaging branding.
description: Wayfinder Stencil CF pairs dramatic curves and strong stroke contrast with stencil cuts across 14 styles from Thin to Heavy. The family is built for logos, headlines and packaging where the interrupted strokes read as a design feature rather than a constraint. Stylistic alternates, swash characters and oldstyle figures extend its range for more expressive typographic applications.
foundry_blurb: Connary Fagen describe Wayfinder Stencil CF as a stencil serif built for branding, logos and headlines, with dramatic curves and contrast suited to fashion, tech, art, food and packaging. Seven weights and matching italics give a wide range of elegant applications, with language support spanning over 25 Latin-script languages.
